overall_summary: Antora Energy, a thermal energy storage developer of highly flexible curtailable loads, urges FERC to require all transmission providers to offer permanent non-firm transmission service at cost-causation rates that reflect actual use, with curtailment integrated into the interconnection study and resource adequacy process. It argues that SPP's one-to-one DNR matching requirement is an insurmountable barrier for flexible loads, that SPP's temporary CHILLS product is insufficient, and that SPP's developing PALS framework should be the national model.

quotes.text: load flexibility can critically help to address the challenge of large load growth by increasing transmission and generation capacity utilization, putting downward pressure on rates, and by allowing the grid to accommodate new loads while infrastructure to serve them is being built.
quotes.concern: flexible load helps absorb large load growth and lower rates

quotes.text: existing service models sharply constrain the commercial viability of flexible load development, and in particular development of highly flexible loads like TES, by treating them the same as inflexible loads.
quotes.concern: current service models penalize flexible loads by treating them like firm loads

quotes.text: Antora urges the Commission, in crafting its remedy in this proceeding, to go beyond these recent reforms to require all transmission providers to: (1) offer permanent non-firm transmission service; (2) at rates reflecting actual use that is consistent with cost causation principles; (3) with curtailment requirements for non-firm load integrated into the interconnection study process and resource adequacy requirements.
quotes.concern: core ask: permanent non-firm service, cost-causation rates, integrated curtailment

quotes.text: Highly flexible large loads face substantial interconnection barriers where, as in SPP, the RTO requires a one-to-one matching of non-coincident peak demand with new Designated Network Resources (DNRs).
quotes.concern: SPP DNR matching requirement blocks flexible load interconnection

quotes.text: An artificial DNR requirement is prohibitively expensive, unnecessary given the real-world operation of curtailable large loads like TES, and acts as a roadblock to new beneficial flexible loads.
quotes.concern: DNR requirement is unnecessary and prohibitively costly for curtailable loads

quotes.text: MISO allows large loads that do not increase the coincident peak to interconnect without new capacity requirements.
quotes.concern: MISO offers a workable flexible-load interconnection model

quotes.text: FERC should require all jurisdictional RTOs and transmission providers to move quickly to develop and implement similar viable interconnection pathways and affordable (based on cost causation) service for large, flexible and curtailable loads.
quotes.concern: FERC should mandate flexible-load pathways across all RTOs

quotes.text: Requiring such loads to procure generation capacity as though they consume around the clock yields unjust and unreasonable costs, contradicts cost-causation principles, and unduly discriminates against such loads by treating differently-situated customers the same.
quotes.concern: forcing capacity procurement on flexible loads violates cost causation

quotes.text: CHILLS is conditional and temporary, only available for a seven-year term, after which the customer must transition to firm service.
quotes.concern: SPP CHILLS is only temporary and forces a later firm transition

quotes.text: CHILLS' temporary structure mirrors the Interim NITS product that the Commission required PJM to establish in its recent Co-Location Order, but does not fill the additional need the Commission identified in that proceeding, for the transmission provider to offer a permanent non-firm transmission service option.
quotes.concern: temporary products fail the need for permanent non-firm service identified in PJM Co-Location Order

quotes.text: Antora proposed an hourly utilization-based rate. This would preserve revenue from high load-factor customers while letting low load-factor loads pay only for the service they use, but this approach was not adopted for CHILLS.
quotes.concern: hourly utilization-based rate would charge flexible loads only for actual use

quotes.text: PALS would create a long-term, non-firm transmission service enabling flexible, price-sensitive loads to interconnect without matching capacity.
quotes.concern: SPP PALS is the right long-term non-firm model without capacity matching

quotes.text: Non-Firm Transmission Charges: Per-MWh billing at hourly non-firm rates (Zonal Annual Transmission Revenue Requirement/Transmission Zone Peak Load/8760) reflect the true cost causation profile of curtailable loads; directly supporting NITS costs reduction for existing ratepayers.
quotes.concern: per-MWh non-firm rates reflect cost causation and cut existing ratepayer costs

quotes.text: this service should be available to any customer capable of meeting the necessary withdrawal limitations, whether or not co-located.
quotes.concern: non-firm flexible service should not be limited to co-located loads

quotes.text: every jurisdictional RTO and transmission owner should be required to offer comparable, permanent, cost-causation-based non-firm service for flexible loads.
quotes.concern: FERC should require comparable permanent non-firm service nationally

bins.name: Permanent non-firm service for flexible loads
bins.description: Antora's central ask is that FERC require all transmission providers to offer permanent, long-term non-firm transmission service for fully curtailable, price-responsive loads, available whether or not the load is co-located. It argues existing models harm flexible loads by treating them like inflexible firm loads, and that load flexibility increases grid utilization and lowers rates.

bins.name: Cost-causation rates reflecting actual use
bins.description: Antora argues transmission charges for curtailable loads should follow cost causation and reflect actual hourly use, such as a per-MWh non-firm rate, rather than forcing flexible loads to pay as though they run around the clock. It says capacity and DNR procurement requirements yield unjust costs and that proper rates reduce NITS costs for existing ratepayers.

bins.name: Integrate curtailment into study and RA
bins.description: Antora asks that the non-firm, curtailable nature of flexible loads be built into the interconnection study process and resource adequacy requirements so study assumptions reflect actual operating profiles rather than full-capacity continuous use.

bins.name: Service not limited to co-located loads
bins.description: Antora notes that the permanent non-firm service it seeks parallels the interim and permanent products from the PJM Co-Location Order, but insists the service must be available to any customer able to meet withdrawal limits, whether or not it is co-located with generation.

bins.name: SPP DNR barrier, CHILLS, and PALS
bins.description: Antora uses its SPP experience as the central case study. It criticizes SPP's one-to-one DNR matching requirement and the temporary, annual-reservation CHILLS product as inadequate, while praising SPP's developing Price Adaptive Load Service as a sound long-term non-firm model that should be completed promptly.

bins.name: MISO as a flexible-load model
bins.description: Antora points to MISO as a region whose rules better accommodate flexible loads, allowing large loads that do not increase the coincident peak to interconnect without new capacity requirements, which is why its first commercial project is in MISO rather than SPP.

bins.name: PJM Co-Location Order precedent
bins.description: Antora cites the Commission's PJM Co-Location Order as recognizing the need for a permanent non-firm transmission service option, and argues temporary products like SPP CHILLS fail to meet that identified need.
